Tarris Reed Jr..strengths: Reed is a walking building code violation at nearly 6'11" with a 7'4.25" wingspan and a 9'2" standing reach — numbers that would make a giraffe jealous … He has the frame of someone who bench-presses small cars and uses that strength to establish post position like he's reserving a table at a packed restaurant … His post game is his bread and butter, and he's developed into a go-to scorer down low who converts at 62.8% from the field because why would you ever leave the paint? … Pulled down a comically dominant 31-point, 27-rebound game against Furman in the NCAA Tournament that looked like a grown man playing against middle schoolers … Averaged 2.0 blocks per game because his 9'2" reach means he can swat your shot while still having a conversation with the guy next to him … Nimble feet for a giant, showing agility that suggests he may have secretly been a dancer in a past life … A major lob threat who presents a target so big that guards could hit him with their eyes closed …
Tarris Reed Jr..weaknesses: His perimeter skill set is about as developed as my ability to speak Klingon — essentially nonexistent beyond the paint … Free throw shooting at 58.6% means late-game situations require a prayer candle and some deep breathing when he's at the line … Not particularly quick or explosive by NBA standards, which is a polite way of saying he moves through quicksand compared to NBA athletes … Can struggle defending in space, which in a switch-heavy NBA is like bringing a knight in armor to a drone warfare fight … Offensive game is confined to the interior, making him scheme-dependent in the way a fish is dependent on water … Older prospect who turns 23 shortly after the draft, which in prospect years is basically retirement age … His game is traditional in a league that's increasingly gone full small-ball mad scientist …
Tarris Reed Jr..outlook: Reed projects as an interior-oriented big who'll grab boards, block shots, and score efficiently without needing the ball — think Isaiah Stewart with more dad jokes … His strength, motor, and presence in the paint give him a path to minutes as an energy big and situational enforcer, like a bouncer who only works specific clubs … His lack of shooting and perimeter mobility cap his upside in today's spacing-obsessed NBA, but rebounding and defense still travel … By all accounts a bright, affable kid who probably says "please" and "thank you" after posterizing you, Reed should earn a role through sheer consistency and effort … Projects in the early-to-mid second round, where teams love taking guys who make the opposing center reconsider his life choices …

Chris Cenac Jr..strengths: Cenac is a 6'11" big man who combines size with the fluidity of someone who just discovered he's tall and decided to lean into it … Runs the floor like a deer that accidentally got stuck in a center's body, finishing above the rim with bounce that makes opposing coaches wince … Averages 7.9 rebounds in just 24.8 minutes because he attacks the glass with the enthusiasm of a kid finding Easter eggs … Shows flashes of a modern offensive skill set including face-up ability, a jab-step pull-up, and developing three-point touch — the offensive equivalent of a highlight reel that's still being edited … His background as a point guard in his youth shows in his ball handling, like a big man who secretly thinks he's a guard and sometimes pulls it off … Active and engaged defensively in Houston's system under Kelvin Sampson, which is basically basketball boot camp with a great track record …
Chris Cenac Jr..weaknesses: Cenac remains raw in the way sushi is raw — it could be amazing or give you problems, depending on who's handling it … Averaged just 9.0 points per game with 48.5% shooting, 33.3% from three, and 62.1% from the line, numbers that look like a scatter plot of someone figuring things out in real time … Post game is underdeveloped, lacking the footwork and counters to consistently score with his back to the basket … His 0.5 blocks per game is underwhelming for a guy his size who should be sending shots into the third row … Perimeter shooting is promising but inconsistent, like a weather forecast that changes every hour … Overall feel and consistency lag behind his physical tools, which is code for "the hardware is great but the software needs updates" …
Chris Cenac Jr..outlook: Cenac projects as a mid-first-round upside swing — the kind of pick that makes GMs look like geniuses or get them fired … His combination of size, fluidity, and emerging perimeter skill fits the modern NBA like stretch pants at a buffet … In the right system with patience, he has the tools to grow into a versatile two-way contributor, assuming the light bulb eventually clicks on … He's a boom-or-bust prospect, which is draft-speak for "might be a star, might be out of the league in four years, and nobody really knows which" …

Joshua Jefferson.strengths: Jefferson is a 6'9", 240-pound forward who plays like a point guard who woke up one morning and just kept growing … His passing is genuinely elite for his size — he drops bounce passes on a dime like he's paying a toll, manipulates defenders like a puppet master, and reads the floor like he's got a cheat sheet taped to his wrist … Averaging 16.4 points, 7.4 rebounds, 4.8 assists, and 1.6 steals while maintaining strong efficiency, because why settle for doing one thing well when you can do everything decently? … His 2:1 assist-to-turnover ratio (4.8 to 2.5) proves he makes good decisions, which is more than can be said for my fantasy basketball draft picks … Excels in pick-and-roll with the patience of a man who's never been in a hurry in his life … Has developed into a capable floor spacer at 34.5% from three, just enough to keep defenders honest without being cocky about it … Disruptive defensively with 1.6 steals per game, using anticipation like he read the scouting report before the other team did …
Joshua Jefferson.weaknesses: Jefferson is not an elite athlete — a polite way of saying his vertical leap is measured in inches rather than fear … His build is somewhat unorthodox with a longer torso and shorter legs, giving him the proportions of a Lego figure and impacting his fluidity … Lacks an explosive first step, relying more on strength and craft than burst, which works in college like a reliable sedan but won't turn heads on the NBA autobahn … Already 23 years old, which in draft years makes him a senior citizen … Only 34.5% from three and 70% from the line, so he's more "please respect me" than "please guard me at all times" from deep … Can be methodical offensively, which is a kind way of saying he might need a shot clock violation warning from time to time …
Joshua Jefferson.outlook: Jefferson has generated late first-round buzz as a connective forward who can facilitate, defend multiple positions, and contribute without needing the ball in his hands every possession — the basketball equivalent of a Swiss Army knife … Shares similarities with Josh Giddey in vision and size, minus the same speed and athleticism, like Giddey's less flashy cousin who still crashes the family dinner … Teams value his plug-and-play skill set as a high-level glue guy, which is basketball's way of saying "he makes everyone around him better while doing the little things that don't show up on Instagram highlights" … Continued shooting improvement and defensive consistency will determine if he's a rotation player or a really good Instagram follow …

Alex Karaban.strengths: Karaban is an elite perimeter shooter who converts at a scorching 48.2% from the field, 39.4% from three, and 84.9% from the line — numbers that would make a sniper nod in approval … His shooting mechanics are unorthodox in the way that some artists paint with garbage — you're not sure how it works, but you can't argue with the results … He possesses deep range and the kind of confidence that makes him think every shot is going in (and he's usually right) … Thrives playing off movement, using screens like a getaway driver and punishing defensive breakdowns with ruthless efficiency … Despite athletic limitations that would make a tortoise feel superior, he gets his shot off using craft, fadeaways, and an arsenal of "how did he do that" moves … Solid passer who doesn't stop the ball, keeps the offense flowing like a well-maintained river, and sports a 6'8" frame with a reported 6'11" wingspan that helps him compensate for being perpetually in quicksand …
Alex Karaban.weaknesses: Karaban's athletic limitations are significant — he plays upright like he's waiting for a bus, lacks fluidity like he's wearing snow pants, and has the foot speed of someone reading terms and conditions … Below-average leaping ability means his margin for error around the rim is thinner than a cheap napkin … Struggles to create offense for himself against anyone with functioning lateral movement, and his game can become stagnant as he parks beyond the arc like a car waiting for its owner … Defensive versatility is limited in the way a bicycle is limited on a highway — he's going to get targeted and he knows it … His overall athletic profile is well below NBA standards, which is draft-speak for "please hide him on defense" … Production plateaued at 13.3 PPG as a senior, raising questions about how much more he has to give …
Alex Karaban.outlook: Karaban projects as a shooting specialist whose value is directly tied to his ability to space the floor like he's claiming territory in a board game … His shooting profile gives him a pathway to stick in the Jason Kapono mold — run off screens, catch, shoot, repeat, collect paycheck … There are shades of Peja Stojaković in his high-release shooting motion, though Peja was slightly less likely to be hunted on defense like wounded prey … He'll need to avoid being a defensive liability, which is the polite way of saying teams will try to ISO him into next week … His success is highly situation-dependent — he needs the right system like a houseplant needs the right window … Projects in the mid second round, where teams love taking specialists and hoping the defense thing works itself out …

Koa Peat.strengths: Peat has a strong, well-built 6'8", 235-pound frame that lets him play through contact like a battering ram in basketball shorts … Brings a blue-collar mentality with the kind of toughness that makes you think he'd win a fight against a grizzly bear (or at least make it work for dinner) … Plays with solid feel and instincts, relying on effort and activity rather than creativity — the basketball equivalent of "work smarter, not harder, but also work harder than everyone else" … Operates effectively as a passer from the high post with 2.7 assists per game, finding cutters like he's got eyes in the back of his head … Efficient scorer at 53.7% who picks his spots like he's choosing seats on an airplane — no nonsense, gets the job done … Earned NCAA Tournament West Region Most Outstanding Player honors because he saves his best for when the lights are brightest and the pressure is highest …
Koa Peat.weaknesses: Defense is a notable concern — struggles to guard in space due to foot speed that can best be described as "committed" rather than "quick" … Undersized for guarding elite interior players and lacks the length and vertical pop to consistently contest shots, which is a problem when you're asked to guard guys who are 7 feet of pure athleticism … Athleticism does not stand out in a league where everyone is an alien — he's more "really good high school player" than "God created a new species" in terms of explosiveness … Outside shooting is questionable at 31.6% from three and 61.6% from the line, with mechanics that break down beyond 12 feet like a car that can't handle highway speeds … His offensive game relies more on strength and effort than dynamic tools, which caps his ceiling in a league that loves the spectacular …
Koa Peat.outlook: One of the most decorated prospects to come out of Arizona, with a strong case as the greatest high school player in state history — his trophy case probably needs its own room … Peat projects as a high-floor, glue-guy forward who contributes through toughness and versatility, like the friend who's always there to help you move apartments … His passing and team-first mentality give him a clear pathway to an NBA role if he keeps his head down and his motor running … Key piece in Arizona's Final Four run, showing his ability to impact winning in the biggest moments … On the first-round bubble, though early-season hype could lift him higher than expected, like a stock that got a good mention on a financial news show … His shooting development will determine if he's a rotation piece or a guy who guards chairs on the bench …

Richie Saunders.strengths: Saunders is one of the premier perimeter marksmen in the 2026 draft class — he shoots 43.2% from three like he's playing Horse and you're losing badly … Averaged 18.0 points per game as a senior for BYU, which is impressive even before you account for the altitude adjustment everyone talks about … Deadly catch-and-shoot weapon who punishes defenses for losing track of him like a teacher who remembers you didn't do the homework … Knocked down 38.7% from three for his career and ranks among BYU's all-time leaders in three-point makes, which in Cougar lore is the equivalent of a religious experience … Quick, compact release that lets him get shots off against pressure — he doesn't need much space, just a hint of daylight and a prayer … Shows strong feel for the game and moves without the ball like he's playing the world's most aggressive game of tag … Defensive instincts are strong with 1.7 steals per game, because even shooters need hobbies …
Richie Saunders.weaknesses: Saunders lacks the elite physical tools typically associated with NBA shooting guards — his first step is more "firm handshake" than "lightning strike" and he struggles to create separation against athletic defenders … Overall athleticism grades as average by NBA standards, which is draft-speak for "he'll need to be smarter than everyone else to make up for it" … Coming off a significant knee injury that introduces the kind of uncertainty that makes GMs nervous and medical staffs reach for extra coffee … Already 24 years old, which in draft years makes him the wise elder statesman who remembers when prospects his age were considered normal … May struggle against NBA length and athleticism when forced into self-creation, like a poet asked to arm-wrestle a bodybuilder …
Richie Saunders.outlook: Saunders projects as an early second-round selection and one of the safer role-player bets in the draft — the kind of guy who shows up, hits shots, and doesn't make anyone question their life choices … BYU's drop-off after his injury showed just how important he was, like realizing your phone battery was what kept your whole day running smoothly … Expected to be back by the NBA season, he profiles as a rotational wing who provides instant floor spacing and the kind of mature game that coaches love and highlight reels ignore … Teams seeking shooting and a developed game could view him as a plug-and-play contributor — microwave scoring off the bench, extra shot of experience included … His recovery and age limit his ceiling, but the shooting is real, and in the NBA, shooting is a get-out-of-jail-free card …

Isaiah Evans.strengths: Evans has one of the purest shooting strokes in the class — the kind of effortless, repeatable motion that makes you think he was born with a basketball in one hand and a net in the other … A highly confident offensive player who never lacks belief in himself — if confidence was a currency, he'd be a billionaire … Dangerous high-volume three-point shooter at 36.2% on 7.5 attempts per game, because why shoot a good shot when you can shoot a great shot from way downtown? … Excellent free throw shooter at 85.7%, reinforcing that his touch is real and not just a product of taking enough shots to eventually make some … Has deep range and gets his shot off with minimal space thanks to a long 6'6" frame with a plus-4 to plus-5 wingspan — he can shoot over you, around you, and possibly through you … Showed real improvement defensively under Jon Scheyer at Duke, learning that defense is not just a suggestion but actually part of the game … Has a natural scoring instinct that makes him hunt points like a predator who just skipped lunch …
Isaiah Evans.weaknesses: Evans has a very thin frame at 180 pounds — he's built less like an NBA wing and more like a tall piece of spaghetti that might snap in a strong wind … Struggles to finish through contact because when you weigh as much as a large dog, bigger players tend to move you out of the way … Lacks elite foot speed and burst, relying on tough shot-making rather than blowing by defenders — the hard way, always the hard way … Assist-to-turnover ratio of 1.3 to 1.1 reflects a scorer's mindset: why pass when you can shoot, and if you can't shoot, maybe just shoot anyway? … Lack of strength limits him defensively against stronger wings, like bringing a butter knife to a sword fight … Questions remain about his all-around game when his shot isn't falling — can he impact winning, or is he just a really expensive light show? …
Isaiah Evans.outlook: Evans's value will be heavily tied to his shooting ability, which in today's NBA is like saying a fish's value is tied to its ability to swim — it's kind of everything … His combination of size, length, and perimeter scoring instincts makes him highly coveted, because teams will always overpay for guys who can shoot … Showed significant year-over-year improvement at Duke, which is what scouts want to see — growth, not just the same highlight package two years in a row … His development hinges on adding strength, expanding his offensive versatility, and proving he can hold his own defensively — in other words, becoming a complete basketball player instead of a really good one-trick pony … At minimum, his elite shooting gives him a pathway to being a rotation wing and floor spacer, which pays very well and comes with excellent hotel accommodations on road trips … Projects as a late lottery to mid first-round pick, because scoring is hard to find and shooting is harder …

Meleek Thomas.strengths: Thomas is one of the more electric offensive guards in the 2026 class — a walking bucket with a microwave scoring mentality that goes from cold to scorching faster than a Hot Pocket … Averaged 15.6 points as a freshman with hilarious efficiency splits of 43.5% from the field, 41.6% from three, and 84.3% from the line — numbers that made Arkansas fans forget about everything else going on in the world … Shot a staggering 48.7% from three during SEC play, which is like shooting 60% in a regular game except everyone guarding you is bigger, faster, and meaner … Excels attacking closeouts with burst and shiftiness that makes defenders look like they're stuck in mud … Despite being seen as a pure scorer, he flashed combo guard ability with 2.5 assists against just 1.0 turnovers — an outstanding ratio for a freshman that suggests he might actually share the ball if you ask nicely … A very good lob passer with touch and timing, because sometimes scoring yourself gets boring and you need to involve others in the fun …
Meleek Thomas.weaknesses: Thomas's game sometimes lacks polish — he can play at a pace that suggests he's being chased by bees, operating at 100 mph without always processing what's in front of him … At moments he resembles a chicken with its head cut off, rushing possessions and forcing offense like the shot clock is a personal insult … Shot selection remains inconsistent, particularly inside the arc, where he has a tendency to take the kind of shots that make coaches pull their hair out … Lacks a reliable floater game, which becomes a problem when bigger defenders meet him at the rim like bouncers at an exclusive club … Arkansas's up-tempo system catered heavily to his strengths, raising the uncomfortable question: was he great, or was he just in the perfect environment? … Decision-making still needs refinement, especially when defense speeds him up and turns his turbo mode into a liability … There are legitimate questions about how much of his efficiency was helped by sharing the floor with Darius Acuff Jr., who drew defensive attention like a magnet …
Meleek Thomas.outlook: Thomas projects as a mid-to-late first-round prospect with intriguing long-term upside, though he may need patience and seasoning like a steak that's not quite done yet … His scoring instincts, shooting, and athleticism give him real offensive upside as an instant-offense combo guard — think Leandro Barbosa with more caffeine and less fear … Encouraging flashes of playmaking add another dimension, but the biggest swing factor is his discipline: if he slows down the game in his head, his ceiling goes way up … If he improves his polish and learns to harness his speed instead of being controlled by it, Thomas has the talent to outperform his draft slot and become a highly productive scoring guard … Or he could stay wild and be the most exciting player on a bad team — either way, it'll be entertaining …

Trevon Brazile.strengths: Brazile is a lanky 6'10" forward with a near 7'4" wingspan that makes him look like a pterodactyl in basketball shorts — he creates disruption defensively just by existing near the basket … Averaged 1.6 blocks as a senior and 1.4 over his career, because when your arms are that long, blocking shots is less a skill and more a natural consequence of being in the area … His switchability is highly regarded for a big man — he can guard on the perimeter and disrupt passing lanes like a spider monitoring its web … Thrives as an energetic lob threat who finishes above the rim with the kind of authority that makes highlight reels and opposing coaches sad … Shot a career 35.1% from three, including 34.1% on 3.7 attempts as a senior, showing he can step out and space the floor when he feels like it … Had signature games against elite competition, including 24 points, 10 rebounds, and 2 blocks against JT Toppin and Texas Tech — proving he can hang with the big dogs when he's locked in …
Trevon Brazile.weaknesses: Brazile lacks consistency throughout 40 minutes — he's impactful in stretches rather than sustained periods, like a wifi signal that keeps cutting out … His ball handling is extremely limited and he plays too loose in traffic, turning the ball over like he's distributing gifts … Has incredible athleticism but doesn't always use his size advantage, averaging just 6 rebounds per game over his career for a 6'10" guy with a 7'4" wingspan — that's like having a Ferrari and never taking it out of first gear … Settles for too many pull-up jumpers when he could be dominating inside, leaving points on the board like money forgotten in an old coat pocket … Can be defensively overaggressive and get into foul trouble, because when you're long and twitchy, you sometimes can't help yourself … Free throw shooting improved to 71.7% but was a grim 52.9% the year before — consistency is not his middle name …
Trevon Brazile.outlook: Brazile has been an under-the-radar prospect his entire career since committing to Missouri, transferring to Arkansas, and slowly turning potential into production … His athleticism and rim-running ability make him stand out, like a talented musician who only plays well when the mood strikes him … He's shown he can rise to the challenge against top competition, but the question is whether he can do it every night instead of just when the stars align … His three-point shooting has remained a constant, which is more than can be said for literally everything else about his game … He'll need to improve his consistency on offense, but his defense and energy should give him a role — think of him as a luxury item who needs the right system to function properly …

Baba Miller.strengths: Miller is one of the most physically intriguing frontcourt prospects in the draft — standing 7 feet with a massive 9'3" standing reach and moving with the fluidity of a player a foot shorter, which is the basketball equivalent of finding a unicorn that also plays the piano … Handles the ball in traffic and operates in face-up situations with the comfort of a point guard trapped in a center's body … His passing is genuinely impressive — he averaged 3.7 assists per game as a senior at Cincinnati, finding cutters and making inventive reads that make you wonder why he doesn't just do this all the time … Averaged 10.3 rebounds per game using length, timing, and activity level — the man can clean the glass when he remembers he's 7 feet tall … His defensive tools are mouth-watering: length, mobility, coordination — the raw ingredients of a good defender, even if the cooking instructions are still being written … Shot 34.2% from three during his junior season at Florida Atlantic, flashing enough potential to keep scouts coming back for more …
Baba Miller.weaknesses: Consistency has been the theme of Miller's career — and not in a good way … He often seems to have a mental block preventing him from fully using his considerable gifts, like a sports car driven by someone who never leaves first gear … Perhaps the most frustrating part: he'll show flashes of brilliance, then disappear for long stretches like a magician who's too good at his own tricks … Played at three different college programs in four seasons — Florida Atlantic, Florida State, Cincinnati — which is either "gaining life experience" or "having trouble committing to one restaurant's menu" … His three-point shooting regressed to a ghastly 19.2% as a senior, and his career 29% from deep with 65.8% free throw shooting raises real questions about whether he can actually shoot or was just faking it for a semester … Can be passive and doesn't impose himself on games, often appearing dependent on situation like a plant that needs just the right light, water, and soil to grow …
Baba Miller.outlook: Miller enters the draft as an older, high-upside reclamation project — the kind of player who makes front offices argue about whether they can fix him or whether they're wasting a pick … After four years of college and three program stops, there's still a sense that his best basketball might be ahead of him, which is either exciting or concerning depending on your tolerance for uncertainty … His blend of size, fluidity, and passing feel makes him an intriguing developmental gamble for organizations with strong player development — the basketball equivalent of a fixer-upper house with great bones … Could emerge as a second-round steal if he lands in the right situation with patient coaches who believe in him … Think Christian Wood's path — maddening inconsistency followed by flashes of brilliance, with the outcome depending entirely on whether something finally clicks …

Ryan Conwell.strengths: Conwell is one of the more accomplished scoring guards in the draft — a left-handed shooter with a smooth, effortless release that makes the net move like it's greeting an old friend … As a senior at Louisville, he averaged 18.8 points, 4.8 rebounds, and 2.7 assists while drilling 112 three-pointers, because apparently shooting is just something he does in his spare time … Shot 37.6% from three for his career on 6.7 attempts per game and 83.6% from the line — numbers that make NBA scouts sit up straight and take notes … His compact shooting motion requires almost no space, allowing him to get shots off against pressure like a contortionist in a phone booth … Much more than a spot-up shooter: his 214.8-pound frame, very good handle, and 39.5-inch max vertical at the NBA Combine (yes, a 6'2" guy almost touched the ceiling) let him attack the rim with violence and finish above it … He's a fearless scorer who thrives on big shots and has multiple 30-point games, suggesting he has the clutch gene that can't be taught … Underrated passer too, with quality vision in pick-and-roll — he's not just a gunner, he's a gunner who sometimes shares …
Ryan Conwell.weaknesses: The main concern is his size — he measured 6'2" barefoot with an 8'0.5" standing reach, which is undersized for a shooting guard and makes him a tweener: not big enough for SG, not quite a pure PG … While his 6'7" wingspan helps, he's still giving up height to almost everyone he'll guard … His three-point shooting dipped to 34.5% as a senior after being over 40% in previous seasons, raising the question: did defenses figure him out, or was it just a blip? … Can occasionally get too aggressive offensively, forcing shots and turning the ball over — his confidence is a superpower until it becomes kryptonite … His lack of size could diminish his explosive athleticism against longer NBA defenders, like a fast car that's still too small to win a demolition derby … He'll need to prove he can create and finish against the length, size, and athleticism of the league, which is the polite way of saying "the NBA is full of giants and you are not one of them" …
Ryan Conwell.outlook: Conwell projects as an early-to-mid second-round selection who should draw interest from teams needing scoring, shooting, and experienced backcourt depth — there's always a market for guys who can put the ball in the basket … His ability to score at all three levels, create his own shot, and handle the ball gives him more versatility than your typical specialist, which is like finding out your favorite takeout place also does delivery and catering … There's some Vinnie "Microwave" Johnson energy to his game — the confidence and scoring instincts to change a game's momentum faster than a plot twist in a thriller … His size likely limits his upside, but his shooting, toughness, and experience give him a realistic chance to carve out a bench scoring role and potentially outperform his draft position — which is really all you can ask from a second-round pick …

Braden Smith.strengths: Smith is a true floor general — the kind of point guard who runs an offense like a chess master who also happens to be really good at yelling "Purdue!" … A genuine coach-on-the-floor who plays with a cerebral approach and a feel for controlling tempo that makes you wonder if he has a PhD in point guard-ology … Finished as the NCAA's all-time assist leader, which is like being the all-time leading scorer in a math competition — it means he's really, really good at setting other people up … Elite passer with timing, vision, and accuracy that allow him to deliver passes in windows so tight they'd make a tow truck driver sweat … His 3-to-1 assist-to-turnover ratio across his career suggests he values the ball like it's his firstborn child … Reliable perimeter shooter at 38.5% from three for his career and 83.2% from the line, proving he can both create for others and punish defenses for ignoring him … Despite being undersized, he started all 149 games at Purdue — durability, consistency, and the kind of trust from coaches that you can't buy at any store … His 6'3.5" wingspan helps offset his height, like a short guy who's learned to reach the top shelf by being clever about it …
Braden Smith.weaknesses: Smith is undersized for an NBA point guard with a thin frame — at his size, finishing inside against NBA centers is like trying to score a try in rugby against a pack of angry forwards … Lacks elite burst and athleticism, limiting his ability to create separation against the kind of defenders who make you question your career choices … Older prospect who turns 23 shortly after the draft, which in prospect years makes him the wise grandfather of the class … Can be targeted defensively by bigger guards who see him and instinctively think "breakfast" … His 14.3 points per game as a senior isn't eye-popping, and he may struggle to generate offense against NBA length — there's a reason they call it the land of giants … His 2.7 turnovers per game reflect occasional overextension, because when you're the best passer on the floor, sometimes you get a little too cute with it …
Braden Smith.outlook: Smith projects as a second-round pick whose appeal is tied to his elite passing, leadership, and polish — the kind of player who makes teammates better and coaches look smart … A steady, reliable floor general who thrives at running a team, playing within structure, and making winning plays — the point guard equivalent of a reliable sedan that always starts in the morning … His combination of feel, toughness, and experience gives him a strong chance to carve out a backup point guard role, stabilize second units, and provide the kind of organizational leadership that doesn't show up in box scores … While his physical limitations cap his ceiling, his intangibles and production make him a strong candidate to outperform his draft position and stick in the league for a decade — which, for a second-round pick, is like hitting the jackpot without buying a ticket …
